DSDS 0.1
Overview Root Schema
example extensions link metadata rich-text status usecase
component pattern style theme token
accessibility anatomy api best-practice design-specifications guideline interaction principle purpose scale state variant

DSDS Best Practices Guideline

Documents actionable usage guidance for an artifact. Each item in the best practices array is a self-contained rule paired with a rationale explaining why. Best practices tell the reader what to do (or not do) and why. They appear alongside other guideline types in the unified guidelines array on any artifact.

Source: guidelines/best-practice.schema.json

2 definitions in this file:

  • bestPracticeEntry
  • bestPractices

bestPracticeEntry

A single, self-contained piece of usage guidance. Every best practice pairs an actionable statement with a rationale explaining why. The optional level classifies enforcement level. The optional category groups by discipline. The optional target identifies which anatomy part or content area the guidance applies to. The optional criteria links to external standards (WCAG, platform guidelines, etc.).

object

PropertyTypeRequiredDescription
guidancerichTextrequiredThe actionable guidance statement. MUST be concrete and unambiguous. Avoid subjective language like 'use sparingly' or 'when possible'.
rationalerichTextrequiredWhy this guidance exists. Cite evidence, accessibility standards, or user research when available. MUST NOT be a restatement of the guidance.
level"required" | "encouraged" | "informational" | "discouraged" | "prohibited"optionalThe enforcement level of the best practice, classifying how strictly it should be followed. Values align with RFC 2119 requirement levels: 'required' (MUST — non-compliance is a defect), 'encouraged' (SHOULD — follow in most cases; exceptions require justification), 'informational' (MAY — advisory context with no enforcement expectation), 'discouraged' (SHOULD NOT — avoid unless justified), 'prohibited' (MUST NOT — violations are defects). When omitted, consumers MAY treat the best practice as 'encouraged'.
categorystringoptionalThe discipline or domain this best practice belongs to. Used to group best practices by professional concern. Standard values: 'visual-design' (color, typography, spacing, layout), 'interaction' (behavior, animation, transitions, gestures), 'accessibility' (inclusive design, assistive technology, WCAG compliance), 'content' (text, imagery, tone, voice, labeling), 'motion' (animation timing, easing, reduced-motion), 'development' (implementation patterns, performance, code conventions). Custom values are permitted and SHOULD be lowercase kebab-case.
targetstringoptionalThe anatomy part or content area this best practice applies to (e.g., 'label', 'icon', 'helper-text'). When omitted, the best practice applies to the artifact as a whole.
criteriastring (uri)[]optionalURLs to external functional requirements or standards that this best practice addresses. For WCAG criteria, use the direct URL to the success criterion (e.g., 'https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#contrast-minimum'). For platform guidelines, link to the specific section.
Format: uri
examplesexample[]optionalExamples illustrating the best practice — showing encouraged and discouraged approaches.
tagsstring[]optionalFreeform keywords to relate best practices across categories and disciplines (e.g., 'rtl', 'localization', 'validation', 'contrast').

References: richText, example

Example

[
  {
    "guidance": "Limit each surface to one primary button.",
    "rationale": "Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. A single primary button directs the user to the most important action.",
    "level": "required",
    "category": "visual-design"
  },
  {
    "guidance": "Place the primary button on the right side of a button group in left-to-right layouts. Place it on the left in right-to-left layouts.",
    "rationale": "Users scan in the direction of the layout's reading order. Placing the primary action at the natural endpoint aligns with the completion point of reading. This convention is also established by operating system dialog patterns.",
    "level": "encouraged",
    "category": "visual-design"
  },
  {
    "guidance": "Do not use a Button when the action navigates the user to a different page or URL. Use a Link component instead.",
    "rationale": "Buttons and links have different semantic roles. Buttons trigger actions (submit, open, close). Links navigate. Screen reader users rely on element role to anticipate behavior. Using a button for navigation creates a mismatch between the announced role and the actual behavior.",
    "level": "prohibited",
    "category": "visual-design"
  },
  {
    "guidance": "Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during asynchronous operations.",
    "rationale": "A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress. The loading state communicates that the action was registered and the system is working. It also prevents double-submission.",
    "level": "encouraged",
    "category": "interaction"
  },
  {
    "guidance": "Use a verb or verb phrase that describes the action the button performs. Two words maximum.",
    "rationale": "Action-oriented labels set clear expectations about what will happen on activation. Short labels prevent truncation on narrow viewports and improve scannability in dense interfaces.",
    "level": "required",
    "category": "content",
    "target": "label",
    "examples": [
      {
        "title": "Good — action-oriented labels",
        "presentation": {
          "type": "code",
          "code": "Save\nAdd item\nSend invite\nDelete\nExport",
          "language": "text"
        }
      },
      {
        "title": "Avoid — vague or non-actionable labels",
        "presentation": {
          "type": "code",
          "code": "Click here\nOK\nYes\nSubmit form data\nGo",
          "language": "text"
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  {
    "guidance": "Use sentence case capitalization.",
    "rationale": "Sentence case is easier to read than title case or all caps. It also localizes more predictably across languages where capitalization rules differ.",
    "level": "required",
    "category": "content",
    "target": "label"
  },
  {
    "guidance": "When using an icon-only button (no visible label), provide an aria-label that describes the action.",
    "rationale": "Screen readers announce button content as the accessible name. Without visible text, there is no accessible name. The aria-label provides one.",
    "level": "required",
    "category": "accessibility",
    "criteria": [
      "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#name-role-value",
      "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#non-text-content"
    ]
  },
  {
    "guidance": "Maintain a minimum tap target of 44x44 CSS pixels for all button sizes.",
    "rationale": "The WCAG 2.5.8 target size criterion requires a minimum 24x24px target, with 44x44px recommended. Touch devices require larger targets to prevent mis-taps. The small button size uses internal padding to meet this requirement even when the visible area is compact.",
    "level": "required",
    "category": "accessibility",
    "criteria": [
      "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#target-size-minimum"
    ],
    "tags": [
      "touch",
      "mobile",
      "target-size"
    ]
  },
  {
    "guidance": "The focus ring must be visible in all color modes (light, dark, high contrast).",
    "rationale": "Keyboard users depend on the focus indicator to track their position. If the focus ring is invisible against the background, navigation becomes impossible.",
    "level": "required",
    "category": "accessibility",
    "criteria": [
      "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#focus-visible",
      "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#focus-not-obscured-minimum"
    ]
  },
  {
    "guidance": "Button label text must meet a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the button background. The button background must meet a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against the surrounding surface.",
    "rationale": "Text contrast ensures readability for users with low vision. Non-text contrast ensures the button boundary is perceivable.",
    "level": "required",
    "category": "accessibility",
    "criteria": [
      "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#contrast-minimum",
      "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#non-text-contrast"
    ]
  }
]

bestPractices

Documents actionable usage rules for an artifact. Each item is a self-contained best practice pairing an actionable guidance statement with a rationale. Best practices can be categorized by discipline (visual-design, accessibility, content, etc.), targeted to specific anatomy parts, and linked to external standards like WCAG. They complement other guideline types — use-cases tell the reader *when* to use the artifact, best practices tell the reader *how* to use it correctly.

object

PropertyTypeRequiredDescription
itemsbestPracticeEntry[]requiredThe best practice rules. Each item is a self-contained guidance statement paired with a rationale. The ordering is significant — tools SHOULD display best practices in this order. Authors SHOULD order by importance or group by category.
Min items: 1
type"best-practices"requiredIdentifies this guideline as a best practices specification.

References: bestPracticeEntry

Example

{
  "type": "best-practices",
  "items": [
    {
      "guidance": "Limit each surface to one primary button.",
      "rationale": "Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. A single primary button directs the user to the most important action.",
      "level": "required",
      "category": "visual-design"
    },
    {
      "guidance": "Place the primary button on the right side of a button group in left-to-right layouts.",
      "rationale": "Users scan in the direction of the layout's reading order. Placing the primary action at the natural endpoint aligns with the completion point of reading.",
      "level": "encouraged",
      "category": "visual-design"
    },
    {
      "guidance": "Do not use a Button when the action navigates the user to a different page or URL. Use a Link component instead.",
      "rationale": "Buttons and links have different semantic roles. Screen reader users rely on element role to anticipate behavior.",
      "level": "prohibited",
      "category": "visual-design"
    },
    {
      "guidance": "Use the loading state instead of disabling the button during asynchronous operations.",
      "rationale": "A disabled button gives no feedback that an action is in progress. The loading state communicates that the action was registered.",
      "level": "encouraged",
      "category": "interaction"
    },
    {
      "guidance": "Use a verb or verb phrase that describes the action the button performs. Two words maximum.",
      "rationale": "Action-oriented labels set clear expectations. Short labels prevent truncation on narrow viewports.",
      "level": "required",
      "category": "content",
      "target": "label"
    },
    {
      "guidance": "When using an icon-only button, provide an aria-label that describes the action.",
      "rationale": "Screen readers need a text equivalent for every interactive control.",
      "level": "required",
      "category": "accessibility",
      "criteria": [
        "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#name-role-value"
      ]
    },
    {
      "guidance": "Maintain a minimum tap target of 44x44 CSS pixels for all button sizes.",
      "rationale": "Touch devices require larger targets to prevent mis-taps. WCAG 2.5.8 requires 24x24px minimum and recommends 44x44px.",
      "level": "required",
      "category": "accessibility",
      "criteria": [
        "https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#target-size-minimum"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
↑ Back to top

Design System Documentation Standard (DSDS) 0.1 — Draft Specification
